title: The Role of Breathwork in Ongoing Psychedelic Integration
body:
The Role of Breathwork in Ongoing Psychedelic Integration

So, you’ve had a psychedelic experience. Maybe it was a single journey or a handful of sessions spaced out over time. Now what? The insights, emotions, and sometimes upheaval don’t just vanish once the effects wear off. That’s where integration comes in, the ongoing work of weaving what you’ve seen and felt into your daily life. Breathwork, believe it or not, can become one of your best tools in this process.

Look, I get it. Breathwork sounds a little woo-woo at first. You might imagine sitting cross-legged, chanting, or something else that feels a bit out there. But honestly, it’s way more accessible than that. Breathwork is simply the conscious use of your breathing patterns to affect your mental, emotional, and physical state. And when you’re working to make sense of psychedelic experiences, that conscious control over breath can be a game changer.

Why Breath Matters After Psychedelics

Right after a psychedelic experience, you’re often flooded with raw emotions, insights, or even confusing sensations. You might feel more sensitive or open, but also a bit unmoored. Breathwork gives you a way to ground yourself in the present moment without trying to push away what you’re feeling. It’s like having a safe anchor when your mind wants to drift off into overwhelming territory.

Here’s the thing, psychedelics can disrupt your usual relationship with your body. You might have felt dissociated during the trip or noticed your heartbeat racing. Breath connects you back to your physical self. It reminds you that you’re here, alive, and able to respond to what’s coming up instead of being swept away by it.

Science backs this up, too. Research from the National Center for Biotechnology Information shows that breathwork can activate the parasympathetic nervous system, which calms your nervous system and reduces stress. That’s exactly what you want when you’re trying to integrate intense psychedelic experiences.

How Breathwork Supports Emotional Processing

If you’re anything like most people I work with, some of your psychedelic experiences stirred up emotions you didn’t expect or weren’t ready to handle. Maybe grief, anger, or deep-seated fear came to the surface. Trying to ‘think’ your way through this is usually a dead end. Breathwork helps you feel those emotions fully without getting overwhelmed.

When you focus on your breath, you give your body permission to express what your mind can’t put into words. It’s like a pressure valve, letting feelings move through you instead of getting stuck. For example, a slow, steady diaphragmatic breath can soothe anxiety, while a more vigorous breath cycle - like the “box breathing” method - can give you energy and focus when you feel stuck in sadness or fatigue.

One person I worked with, Mia, described it this way: “When I tried to talk about what I felt after my last trip, I just shut down. But when I did breathwork, the emotions flowed out like a dam breaking. It was exhausting but also freeing.” Does that land?

Breathwork Techniques to Try for Integration

There’s no shortage of breathwork styles out there, but some are better suited to ongoing psychedelic integration than others. Here are a few you can start with, no special equipment needed:

  • Diaphragmatic Breathing: Lie down or sit comfortably. Place one hand on your belly, one on your chest. Breathe in deeply through your nose so your belly rises, then exhale fully through your mouth. Repeat for five minutes. This calms the nervous system and grounds you.
  • Box Breathing: Inhale for a count of 4, hold for 4, exhale for 4, hold for 4. Repeat 3-5 times. This technique helps regulate your nervous system and improve focus when your mind is scattered.
  • Alternate Nostril Breathing: Use your thumb and ring finger to close one nostril at a time. Inhale through one nostril, exhale through the other. This balances your brain hemispheres and can bring clarity to your thoughts.
  • 4-7-8 Breathing: Inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 7, exhale for 8. Repeat 4 times. This is great for calming anxiety or helping you sleep after a day of heavy integration work.

Not ready to do this alone yet? You can find guided breathwork sessions on apps or YouTube. Or try a gentle meditation timer (paid link) to keep you on track during practice.

Breathwork and Mental Clarity

One of the biggest challenges after psychedelic sessions is feeling foggy or confused. Your brain’s been through a reset of sorts, but it can take time to settle back into daily mental rhythms. Breathwork can sharpen your clarity by increasing oxygen flow to your brain and regulating your heart rate variability.

Try this simple practice when you wake up or feel stuck during the day: breathe deeply and slowly for 2-3 minutes. Pay attention to how your chest and belly move. You might notice your mind clearing a bit - an opening for your integration insights to come through more clearly.

Pair this with other focus-supporting habits. For example, wearing blue light blocking glasses (paid link) in the evening helps your brain wind down and keeps your circadian rhythm steady, making breathwork more effective for calming your system.

Breathwork as a Tool for Trauma Release

Sometimes psychedelics unearth trauma you didn’t realize was there. This is where breathwork really shines. You don’t have to relive trauma verbally or intellectually to process it. Breath allows your body to move through tense places. It’s a somatic way to work through what words can’t reach.

But here’s a caution: if you’re dealing with heavy trauma, don’t try to do breathwork alone without guidance. Finding a therapist or breathwork coach who understands trauma-informed practices can make a huge difference. Breath can open doors, but you want a safe place to step through.

Building Breathwork Into Your Integration Routine

Consistency is key. Breathwork isn’t a one-and-done fix. Try adding a few minutes every day or every other day. You might start your morning with a calming breath session to set your tone, then come back to it in moments of stress or confusion.

Here’s a simple structure to keep your practice sustainable:

  • Set a daily reminder on your phone or use an intermittent fasting tracker (paid link) app to keep you consistent with timing.
  • Choose 1-2 breathwork exercises that fit your needs and stick with them for a week or two.
  • Journal immediately after your breathwork to capture insights or sensations. This creates a feedback loop and deepens your integration.
  • Pair breathwork with other integration tools like gentle movement, creative expression, or sound therapy.

If you want more ideas on structuring your integration practices, check out my article on Building Your Psychedelic Integration Practice. It’s full of practical steps that’ll help you hold space for the work ahead.

When Breathwork Feels Hard

Not every breath session is going to feel amazing. Sometimes, especially early on, breathwork can bring up discomfort, restlessness, or even panic. That’s normal. Your nervous system is waking up, and old patterns may surface.

Here’s what to do if that happens:

  • Slow down your breath. Stop any rapid or forceful breathing.
  • Return to natural, gentle breathing. Close your eyes if it helps.
  • Ground yourself by feeling your feet on the floor or holding a comforting object.
  • Remind yourself that you’re safe and in control.
  • Seek support if the feelings become too much. Breathwork is not a replacement for professional help.

Truth is, challenging breathwork sessions often precede breakthroughs. But don’t push yourself beyond what feels manageable. Breathwork should be a tool you come back to, not a source of distress.

Resources to Support Your Breathwork Practice

Ready to get started? Here are a few resources to help you build and sustain your breathwork practice alongside your psychedelic integration:

  • Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies (MAPS) offers research and guidance on psychedelics and integration.
  • My personal site has ongoing articles, workshops, and tools for breathwork and psychedelic wellness.
  • Books like Stealing Fire (paid link) explore altered states and how to work with them in daily life.

With practice, breathwork can become your anchor, your reset button, and your way to hold space for whatever the integration process throws your way. Remember, you’re not trying to control everything. You’re simply giving yourself a tool to breathe through the chaos, the joy, the confusion - whatever it is.

Know what I mean? Breath is the simplest thing you always have. Use it well.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
